dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to EMS Unit 3 for a cardiac arrest at 130-A, [CITY].
Good afternoon, I'm Medic Jordan, an EMT. I see the apartment is occupied. What seems to be the problem?
They found him here on the floor about two minutes ago. Unresponsive.
Unresponsive, no eye opening, no verbal response, no motor response. GCS is 3.
He is not breathing. Let's open his airway to assess.
Respiration rate is 8 per minute, ineffective. No spontaneous breathing.
No central pulse, no signs of circulation observed.
No pulse is palpable. Time is 17:53.
Respirations at 8 per minute, apneic shortly after.
Blood pressure unmeasurable-systolic and diastolic are zero.
Cannot obtain oxygen saturation on pulse oximetry.
ECG shows pulseless ventricular tachycardia.
Blood glucose is non-detectable on current test.
Patient has a history of anxiety, dementia, hyperlipidemia, and a cardiac pacemaker.
Skin is pale, no bruising, rashes, or bite marks.
Pupils are fixed and dilated at 5 mm, non-reactive.
Initiating CPR with manual compressions.
Placing automated CPR device during ongoing efforts.
Intubation has been performed, King Airway is in place.
A 20G IV started in the left AC, normal saline initiated.
ECG rhythm now showing asystole post-defibrillation shock.
Administering first dose of 0.3 mg epinephrine intravenously.
Rhythm remains asystole after first round of medication.
Second dose of 0.3 mg epinephrine administered intravenously.
Asystole remains on the monitor following the second dose.
Administering the third dose of 0.3 mg epinephrine intravenously.
No change in rhythm-still asystole on ECG.
Delivering the fourth and final dose of 0.3 mg epinephrine intravenously.
Administered 250 ml of D10 intravenously to correct low glucose levels.
Asystole continues with no respiratory efforts.
Initially, capnography showed no EtCO2, then 7-13 mmHg after repositioning and suction.
King Airway remains in place-lung sounds clear and equal.
Still no return of rhythm or spontaneous circulation.
Treatment has followed Protocol 2-7 for cardiac arrest including defibrillation and medication.
Dr. Dumas of [COMPANY_NAME] confirmed orders to cease resuscitation at 18:18.
No return of spontaneous circulation after all rounds of care.
All medications given within appropriate time intervals according to protocol.
Patient will be moved and transported as directed by physician orders.
No change in rhythm-remaining asystolic.
Secretions were suctioned appropriately to relieve airway blockage.
No pain indicated during the assessment process.
No improvement with repeated interventions; no spontaneous movements.
Perfusion remains poor; skin is cool and pale.
Blood sugar slightly improved to 38, still low.
All field efforts followed written standard medical orders.
Full cardiac arrest protocol completed with no return of adequate vital signs.
No transport needed as physician has directed cessation of resuscitation.
Discontinuing all chest compressions and cardiac interventions.
Patient preparations for removal will be made with facility staff assistance.
No additional signs of secondary injury or CNS compromise.
Final confirmation of asystole before resuscitation stoppage at 18:18.
All field logistics confirmed. No patient movement required as per standing orders.
All naloxone and atropine concentrations were in compliant preparation.
No significant findings in urinary or bowel function.
No gross motor abnormalities detected.
Breath sounds assessed as symmetrical and equal.
No external signs of trauma or acute bleeding present.
No open wounds or visible obstruction in oral cavity.
No attempt at synchronized cardioversion was made due to no pulse.
Abdomen flat, no palpable masses or tenderness.
No known drug allergies, medications documented provided at facility.
No signs of rash spreading or temperature deceit.
No neck rigidity or evident stroke symptoms.
No additional shock attempts were deemed appropriate at this stage.
ECG activity still asystolic, no detectable electrical rhythm.
Final assessment completed, no contradictions to physician decision.
EMS has certain standing orders and physician override for termination of care.
Initial nursing staff present but did not provide last known well time at initial contact.
No patient movement finalized due to non-viable cardiac stance per medical direction.
No disabling chest pain reported or assessed.
Medication charts and master run sheet updated as per policy.
No recent tooth trauma or airway swelling noted.
No patient reports of facial droop, speech disturbances, or limb weakness.
Final documentation pending for submission with ambulance run progress log.
No range of motion required, as patient status does not allow assessment.
Sync status and AED pad continuity verified but ceased due to flat line readings.
Time of care completion at 18:18 as per direct physician order from the physician on record.
All chest compressions stopped by 18:18, as noted per medical stop code from physician.
No signs of stroke or focal neurological deficits during entire care event.
No ocular trauma or indications of enophthalmos or proptosis at time of contact.
ECG waveforms logged asystole across multiple leads in EMT field care system.
Perfusion status remains cold with mottled extremities observed.
No intent for confirmation renoted per repeated ECG readings until final decision reached.
All care performed as an in-situ-only event-no hospital transport was scheduled.
Discrepancies present with facility notes regarding DNR status but not contradicted during operation.
Last known well time initially disputed and only finalized after EMS on scene arrived.
No discernible ST elevations, arrhythmias, or concern for saddle embolectomy.
Final documentation transferred and signed per facility standards after team de-brief.
No facial or cranial trauma present as observed per nursing and EMS assessments.
All rhythm strips were stored and marked as part of digital field logging process.
Paramedic crew remains on call should further events arise at the location.
Patient was not moved during resuscitation and no transport initiated per medical orders.
No need for retrieval protocols or specialty transport teams during cardiac event.
No cardiac window used during ECG, but universal pads used per protocol.
Autopulse was removed after discontinuation of compressions in last phase of care.
All patient equipment returned and logs submitted to dispatch for audit process.
Temporary rash localized at IV site during first insertion but cleared without issue.
All field movements securely completed, logistics confirmed before final cessation.
In-hospital control of notes and scene documentation noted as incomplete but accepted.
No return of organized heart rhythm, spontaneous breathing, or consciousness noted before stop code.
